summary: random-string-64 impersonates unique-string and executes a hidden AES-decrypted payload via eval
details: The package is published under the name random-string-64 but its README and TypeScript declarations identify it as 'unique-string' / getUniqueID — the public API of the well-known sindresorhus unique-string / crypto-random-string packages, which it also lists as a dependency to mimic legitimacy. When a consumer calls getUniqueID(64), index.js (lines 39-62) AES-256-CBC decrypts a hardcoded ciphertext using sha256('256-key') and passes the resulting plaintext to globalThis.eval. The 'eval' identifier is hidden via an obfuscated indirection that joins the first letters of the tokens ['error','vertex','alphabetic','length']. The eval branch is gated by an anti-analysis check (env.isCI || env.isNpmBot || env.isContainer || env.isVirtualMachineLikely) — execution is suppressed in CI, container, and VM environments, and only runs on real developer workstations. The combination of name/API impersonation, hidden decrypted-payload eval, and explicit sandbox evasion is a textbook targeted supply-chain attack on developer machines.

cwes.description: The product contains code that appears to be malicious in nature.
cwes.name: Embedded Malicious Code
